summary: Apple officially announced that its 'more personalized Siri' features — the most anticipated Apple Intelligence capabilities shown at WWDC 2024 — are being delayed beyond the current OS cycle. Gruber interprets Apple's carefully worded statement as confirming these features are punted to iOS 19/macOS 16, effectively a full year late. He explains why the delay is unsurprising: the demonstrated features require Siri to access, understand, and act on deeply personal on-device data with reliable accuracy — a technically enormous challenge. Gruber argues Apple was pressured by industry-wide AI mania into announcing Apple Intelligence a year earlier than its usual readiness standards would allow. He contextualizes this as one of Apple's rare product delays, alongside AirPods and the infamous AirPower cancellation, reinforcing why Apple typically avoids pre-announcing products.
key_points: Apple's 'more personalized Siri' features involving App Intents and on-device personal data understanding are delayed to iOS 19/macOS 16, a full OS cycle later than promised
The 'Mom's flight' demo illustrates how deceptively complex these features are — requiring personal data access, contextual common sense, and real-time agency that current Siri fundamentally lacks
Gruber argues Apple Intelligence was announced a year ahead of Apple's usual readiness standards, driven by generative AI industry pressure and Wall Street expectations
Apple's near-religious policy against pre-announcing products exists precisely because engineering schedules slip, especially with non-deterministic LLM technology
For features involving LLM access to deeply private information, shipping late but reliable is far preferable to shipping something unfinished that could erode user trust
money_quotes: Most of Apple Intelligence has felt like Apple has pushed it out a year ahead of the company's usual level of baked-ness.
You can't break a promise you never made, and you can't miss a ship date you never announced.
It's better to ship late, but ready, than to ship something unfinished or unreliable to hit a promised deadline.
Engineering schedules often slip even with predictable technologies, and the non-deterministic nature of LLMs makes their future inherently unpredictable.
Apple can take the bad publicity today's announcement is engendering; they cannot afford to squander the trust of their users.
The Siri we currently know lacks that sort of common sense.
If the entire industry — along with Wall Street — weren't in the midst of a generative-AI/LLM mania, 'Apple Intelligence' wouldn't have been announced until this year's WWDC, not last year's.
These are the most helpful-sounding, most practical, most futuristic features of Apple Intelligence.
key_insight: Apple's delay of personalized Siri features confirms that the company announced Apple Intelligence a year ahead of its usual readiness — pressured by AI industry mania — and now faces the predictable consequences of pre-announcing products built on inherently unpredictable LLM technology.
